p:
Suppose $T$ is a finite set of positive integers. If the greatest integer in $T$ is removed, the average value of the integers remaining is $42$. If the least integer in $T$ is also removed, then the average value of the integers remaining is $46$. If the greatest integer is then returned to the set, the average value of the integers rises to $49$. The greatest integer in the original set $T$ is $81$ greater than the least integer in $T$. What is the average value of all the integers in the set $T$?
A) 44.75  
B) 45.25  
C) 45.75  
D) 46.25

In a survey, the number of pets owned by 19 individuals is recorded. Determine the median number of pets owned. The distribution is as follows:
- 5 individuals own 2 pets
- 6 individuals own 3 pets
- 1 individual owns 4 pets
- 4 individuals own 5 pets
- 3 individuals own 6 pets
A. 2 pets
B. 3 pets
C. 4 pets
D. 5 pets
E. 6 pets

If the product $\dfrac{5}{3} \cdot \dfrac{6}{5} \cdot \dfrac{7}{6} \cdot \ldots \cdot \dfrac{a}{b} = 15$, where $ a $ and $ b $ are consecutive integers with $ a > b $, what is the sum of $ a $ and $ b $?
(A) 77
(B) 85
(C) 89
(D) 95
(E) 99

There are 25 students in a school club where they can participate in chess, music, or art classes. Each student must join at least one of these activities, and it's possible for students to join two or all three activities. There are 12 students in chess, 15 in music, and 11 in art. A total of 11 students are participating in at least two activities. How many students are enrolled in all three activities?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 4\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 5\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 6$
$\textbf{(A)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 4\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 5\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 6$

The Fibonacci-like sequence starts with $2, 3$, and each term afterwards is the sum of its two predecessors. Which one of the ten digits is the last to appear in the units position of a number in this modified Fibonacci sequence?
A) 0
B) 3
C) 6
D) 8
E) 9

Determine the number of distinct rational numbers $k$ such that $|k|<500$ and the equation $$7x^2+kx+18=0$$ has at least one integer solution for $x$.
$\textbf{(A) }100\qquad \textbf{(B) }110\qquad \textbf{(C) }120\qquad \textbf{(D) }130\qquad \textbf{(E) }140\qquad$

Inside a right circular cone with a base radius of $4$ and a height of $15$, three identical spheres are placed such that each is tangent to the others as well as the base and side of the cone. If the center plane of the spheres is at a height $r+1$ from the base, determine the radius $r$ of each sphere.
A) $1.2$
B) $1.5$
C) $2$
D) $2.5$

Dianna's teacher gave her the task to substitute numbers for $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, $e$, and $f$ in the expression $a-(b-(c-(d+(e-f))))$ and compute the result. Dianna misinterpreted the parentheses but performed the addition and subtraction accurately, coincidentally arriving at the correct result. Dianna used the numbers $1$, $2$, $3$, $4$, and $5$ respectively for $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, and $e$. What number did Dianna substitute for $f$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 1 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 2 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 3 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 4$

Which of the following expressions is (are) rational: $\sqrt{e^2},\,\sqrt[3]{0.64},\,\sqrt[4]{0.0256},\,\sqrt[3]{-8}\cdot \sqrt{(.25)^{-1}}$?
- **(A)** None
- **(B)** All
- **(C)** The third and fourth
- **(D)** Only the fourth
- **(E)** Only the third

Let $B$ be the set of positive integers that have no prime factors other than $2$, $3$, $5$, or $7$. Calculate the sum of the reciprocals of the elements of $B$, expressed as a reduced fraction $\frac{m}{n}$ where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime. What is $m+n$?
A) 36
B) 41
C) 43
D) 45
E) 47

A train travels a distance of $x$ kilometers at a speed of 50 mph and then a further distance of $y$ kilometers at a speed of 70 mph. Additionally, the train waits for 30 minutes at a station before completing the second part of its journey. Calculate the total travel time in hours.
A) $\frac{7x + 5y}{350}$
B) $\frac{7x + 5y}{350} + 0.5$
C) $\frac{7x + 5y}{175}$
D) $\frac{7x + 5y}{175} + 0.5$

In a new variant of the game, Jenn and Bela play on a closed interval $[0, n]$ of the real line, where $n$ is a fixed integer greater than $10$. This time, Bela must start at a fixed point $x=3$, and players must choose a number that is at least two units away from any previously chosen number. Determine the winner, assuming both players use optimal strategies.
$\textbf{(A)} \text{ Bela will always win.} \qquad \textbf{(B)} \text{ Jenn will always win.} \qquad \textbf{(C)} \text{ Bela will win if and only if }n \text{ is odd.} \\ \textbf{(D)} \text{ Jenn will win if and only if }n \text{ is odd.} \qquad \textbf{(E)} \text { Jenn will win if and only if } n>12.$
$\textbf{(A)} \text{ Bela will always win.} \qquad \textbf{(B)} \text{ Jenn will always win.} \qquad \textbf{(C)} \text{ Bela will win if and only if }n \text{ is odd.} \\ \textbf{(D)} \text{ Jenn will win if and only if }n \text{ is odd.} \qquad \textbf{(E)} \text { Jenn will win if and only if } n>12.$
ch: text{ Bela will win if and only if }n \text{ is odd.} \\
text{ Jenn will win if and only if }n \text{ is odd.}

text { Jenn will win if and only if } n>12.$
$\textbf{(A)} \text{ Bela will always win.} \qquad \textbf{(B)} \text{ Jenn will always win.} \qquad \textbf{(C)} \text{ Bela will win if and only if }n \text{ is odd.} \\ \textbf{(D)} \text{ Jenn will win if and only if }n \text{ is odd.} \qquad \textbf{(E)} \text { Jenn will win if and only if } n>12.

p:
The base of a triangle is $18$ inches. Three lines are drawn parallel to the base, terminating in the other two sides, and dividing the triangle into four equal areas. The length of the second parallel line closer to the base is:
A. $9\sqrt{2}$ inches
B. $10$ inches
C. $9$ inches
D. $9\sqrt{3}$ inches

A circle has a circumference of $30\pi$. Find the area of this circle in terms of $\pi$.
A. $15\pi$
B. $75\pi$
C. $150\pi$
D. $225\pi$
E. $450\pi$

In $\triangle ABC$, $AD$ and $AE$ trisect $\angle BAC$. The lengths of $BD$, $DE$, and $EC$ are $4$, $2$, and $5$, respectively. The length of the shortest side of $\triangle ABC$ is?
A) $5\sqrt{5}$
B) $9$
C) $11$
D) $\text{not uniquely determined by the given information}$
ch: $\text{not uniquely determined by the given information}

p:
A can of soup can feed $4$ adults or $4$ children. If there are $10$ cans of soup and $20$ children are fed, how many adults can be fed with the remaining soup?
A. 10
B. 15
C. 20
D. 25
E. 30

Given that $\frac{a}{b} < -\frac{c}{d}$, where $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$, and $e$ are real numbers with $b, d > 0$ and $e > 0$. Also given $a + e > 0$, which of the following is true?
- (A) $a$ must be negative
- (B) $a$ must be positive
- (C) $a$ must not be zero
- (D) $a$ can be negative or zero, but not positive
- (E) $a$ can be positive, negative, or zero
- (A) $a$ must be negative
- (B) $a$ must be positive
- (C) $a$ must not be zero
- (D) $a$ can be negative or zero, but not positive
- (E) $a$ can be positive, negative, or zero

The negation of the statement "all cars are fast" is:
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{no cars are fast} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ \text{all cars are not fast} \\ \textbf{(C)}\ \text{some cars are not fast}\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \text{no cars are slow}\\ \textbf{(E)}\ \text{some cars are fast}$

If $y = x^2 + px + q + r$, and the minimum possible value of this modified quadratic function is $-r$, what must the value of $q$ be?
- **(A)** $ q = \frac{p}{2} $
- **(B)** $ q = \frac{p^2}{4} $
- **(C)** $ q = \frac{p^2}{4} - r $
- **(D)** $ q = -\frac{p^2}{4} $
- **(E)** $ q = -r $

Suppose $y_1$ and $y_2$ are roots with $y_1 \neq y_2$ of the equation $2y^2 - ky - m = 0$. Determine the sum $y_1 + y_2$.
$\mathrm{(A) \ } \frac{k}{3} \qquad \mathrm{(B) \ } k \qquad \mathrm{(C) \ } \frac{k}{2} \qquad \mathrm{(D) \ } m \qquad \mathrm{(E) \ } -\frac{m}{2}$

Given the nonzero real numbers $c, d, k,$ and $n$, where $c$ and $k$ are positive, find the conditions under which the solution to $ce^{dx} = 1$ is less than the solution to $ke^{nx} = 1$.
A) $\frac{\ln(c)}{\ln(k)} > \frac{d}{n}$  
B) $\frac{\ln(k)}{\ln(c)} > \frac{n}{d}$  
C) $\frac{\ln(c)}{\ln(k)} < \frac{d}{n}$  
D) $\frac{d}{n} > \frac{\ln(c)}{\ln(k)}$  
E) None of the above

How many $12$-letter arrangements of $4$ A's, $4$ B's, $3$ C's, and $1$ D avoid placing any A in the first $4$ letters, any B in the next $4$ letters, and any C or D in the last $4$ letters?
$\textrm{(A)}\ 144$
$\textrm{(B)}\ 192$
$\textrm{(C)}\ 256$
$\textrm{(D)}\ 320$
$\textrm{(E)}\ 64$

On a board of $ 10 \times 10 $ unit squares, what is the probability that a randomly chosen unit square does not touch the perimeter or any squares in the horizontal and vertical center lines (5th and 6th lines of the board)?
A. $ \frac{2}{5} $
B. $ \frac{1}{2} $
C. $ \frac{3}{5} $
D. $ \frac{7}{10} $
E. $ \frac{4}{5} $

A number $m$ is randomly selected from the set $\{11, 13, 17, 19, 23\}$, and a number $n$ is randomly selected from $\{2001, 2002, \ldots, 2020\}$. What is the probability that $m^n$ has a units digit of $1$?
$\textbf{(A) } \frac{1}{5} \qquad \textbf{(B) } \frac{1}{4} \qquad \textbf{(C) } \frac{3}{20} \qquad \textbf{(D) } \frac{7}{20} \qquad \textbf{(E) } \frac{2}{5}$

If $ x $ is real and positive and grows beyond all bounds, then $ \log_4{(8x-7)}-\log_4{(3x+2)} $ approaches:
(A) 0 (B) 1 (C) 2 (D) $\log_4 \left(\frac{8}{3}\right)$ (E) no finite number

Let $m$ denote the smallest positive integer that is divisible by both $4$ and $9,$ and whose base-$10$ representation consists of only $6$'s and $9$'s, with at least one of each. What are the last four digits of $m?$
$\textbf{(A) } 6996 \qquad\textbf{(B) } 6699 \qquad\textbf{(C) } 9669 \qquad\textbf{(D) } 9966 \qquad\textbf{(E) } 9666$

A bundle of paper containing $400$ sheets is $4$ cm thick. Approximately how many sheets of this type of paper would there be in a stack $6$ cm high?
$\text{(A)}\ 400 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 500 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 600 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 700 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 800$

Let $s$ be the number that results when both the base and the exponent of $c^d$ are quadrupled, where $c,d>0$. If $s$ equals the square of the product of $c^d$ and $y^d$ where $y>0$, then $y=$
A. $4c$
B. $8c$
C. $16c$
D. $32c$
E. $64c$

An open-top box has a square base of side $a$ and height $b$. The total surface area including the bottom and the open top of the box, and its volume are numerically equal. If $1 \le a \le b$, find all possible integer pairs $(a, b)$.
**A) $(5, 5)$ only**  
**B) $(4, 4)$ only**  
**C) $(8, 2)$ and $(5, 5)$**  
**D) $(6, 3)$ only**  
**E) None of these**

There are 25 students in a school club that offers activities in chess, soccer, and music. Each student participates in at least one of these activities. There are 15 students in chess, 18 in soccer, and 10 in music. There are 14 students who are part of at least two activities. Determine how many students are participating in all three activities.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 4\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 5\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 6\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 7$

Square pyramid $FGHIJ$ has base $FGHI$, which measures $4$ cm on a side, and altitude $FJ$ perpendicular to the base, which measures $8$ cm. Point $P$ lies on $GJ$, one fourth of the way from $G$ to $J$; point $Q$ lies on $IJ$, one fourth of the way from $I$ to $J$; and point $R$ lies on $HJ$, three fourths of the way from $H$ to $J$. What is the area, in square centimeters, of $\triangle{PQR}$?
A) $\sqrt{20}$
B) $\sqrt{30}$
C) $\sqrt{35}$
D) $8\sqrt{5}$
E) $12\sqrt{5}$

At Euclid Middle School, the mathematics teachers are Mrs. Germain, Mr. Newton, Mrs. Young, and Mr. Euler. There are $12$ students in Mrs. Germain's class, $10$ students in Mr. Newton's class, $7$ students in Mrs. Young's class, and $6$ students in Mr. Euler's class taking the AMC $8$ this year. How many mathematics students at Euclid Middle School are taking the contest?
**$\textbf{(A)}\ 33 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 34 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 35 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 36 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 37$**

Jessica has $3p + 2$ nickels, and Samantha has $2p + 6$ nickels. Compute the difference in their money in pennies.
- $\textbf{(A)}\ 5p - 20$
- $\textbf{(B)}\ 3p + 2$
- $\textbf{(C)}\ 2p + 6$
- $\textbf{(D)}\ p - 4$
- $\textbf{(E)}\ \text{none of these}$

An octahedron floats in space with a top face and a bottom face, each connected to a middle belt consisting of six connected triangular faces arranged in a ring. How many ways are there to move from the top face to the bottom face such that:
- You must visit each face exactly once.
- Moves can only be made to adjacent faces.
- Moves around the middle belt are restricted to one direction (either clockwise or counterclockwise).
A) 1
B) 2
C) 3
D) 4
E) 5

Rectangle $ABCD$ is inscribed in a semicircle with diameter $\overline{FE}$. Let $DA=20,$ and let $FD=AE=7.$ What is the area of $ABCD?$
A. $60\sqrt{19}$
B. $60\sqrt{20}$
C. $60\sqrt{21}$
D. $60\sqrt{22}$
E. $60\sqrt{23}$

Five friends — Adrian, Brenda, Charlie, Dana, and Evan — went to a fair. Each had a whole number of dollars, and together they had $72$ dollars. The absolute differences between the amounts were as follows: Adrian and Brenda $21$ dollars, Brenda and Charlie $8$ dollars, Charlie and Dana $6$ dollars, Dana and Evan $5$ dollars, and Evan and Adrian $14$ dollars. How many dollars did Evan have?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 16\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 19\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 21\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 23\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 28$

In a grid of letters and numerals, by how many different paths can one spell AMCX? Starting at the 'A' in the middle, a path only allows moves from one letter to an adjacent (above, below, left, or right, but not diagonal) letter. The grid setup is the same, but now you want to end at an 'X' that is adjacent to each 'C'. How many unique paths are there?
$\textbf{(A) }4\qquad\textbf{(B) }8\qquad\textbf{(C) }12\qquad\textbf{(D) }24\qquad\textbf{(E) }36$

How many primes less than $50$ have $3$ as the ones digit?
$\text{(A)} \ 3 \qquad  \text{(B)} \ 4 \qquad  \text{(C)} \ 5 \qquad  \text{(D)} \ 6 \qquad \text{(E)} \ 7$

$D$, $E$, and $F$ are three piles of rocks. The mean weight of the rocks in $D$ is $30$ pounds, the mean weight of the rocks in $E$ is $60$ pounds, the mean weight of the rocks in the combined piles $D$ and $E$ is $45$ pounds, and the mean weight of the rocks in the combined piles $D$ and $F$ is $50$ pounds. What is the greatest possible integer value for the mean in pounds of the rocks in the combined piles $E$ and $F$?
$\textbf{(A)} \ 60 \qquad \textbf{(B)} \ 63 \qquad \textbf{(C)} \ 65 \qquad \textbf{(D)} \ 67 \qquad \textbf{(E)} \ 70$

A regular hexagon has a side length of 8 units. Congruent arcs with radius 4 units are drawn with the center at each of the vertices, creating circular sectors. These sectors span an angle of 120 degrees each. Find the area of the region inside the hexagon but outside the sectors.
A) $ 96\sqrt{3} - 16\pi $
B) $ 64\sqrt{3} - 24\pi $
C) $ 96\sqrt{3} - 24\pi $
D) $ 96\sqrt{3} - 32\pi $
E) $ 128\sqrt{3} - 32\pi $

Each of two boxes contains chips numbered $1$, $2$, $4$, and $5$. A chip is drawn randomly from each box and the numbers on the two chips are multiplied. What is the probability that their product is odd?
$\textbf{(A) }\frac{1}{4}\qquad\textbf{(B) }\frac{1}{2}\qquad\textbf{(C) }\frac{3}{4}\qquad\textbf{(D) }\frac{1}{8}\qquad \textbf{(E) }\frac{3}{8}$

A rectangle has a ratio of length to width of 5:2. If the diagonal of the rectangle is 13 units, determine $k$ such that the area of the rectangle may be expressed as $kd^2$, where $d$ is the diagonal.
- **(A)** $\frac{10}{25}$
- **(B)** $\frac{10}{29}$
- **(C)** $\frac{10}{23}$
- **(D)** $\frac{8}{29}$
- **(E)** $\frac{12}{29}$

Consider three squares where each square is divided differently. Square I is divided by diagonals into four smaller triangles, and one of these triangles is shaded. Square II is divided into four smaller squares by segments connecting the midpoints of the sides, and two of these smaller squares are shaded. Square III is divided by two sets of parallel lines, each set connecting midpoints of opposite sides, creating a grid of sixteen smaller equal squares, with four of them shaded. How do the shaded areas of these squares compare?
$\text{(A)}\ \text{The shaded areas in all three are equal.}$
$\text{(B)}\ \text{Only the shaded areas of }I\text{ and }II\text{ are equal.}$
$\text{(C)}\ \text{Only the shaded areas of }II\text{ and }III\text{ are equal.}$
$\text{(D)}\ \text{Only the shaded areas of }I\text{ and }III\text{ are equal.}$
$\text{(E)}\ \text{The shaded areas of }I, II\text{ and }III\text{ are all different.}$
ch: \text{The shaded areas in all three are equal.}$
\text{Only the shaded areas of }I\text{ and }II\text{ are equal.}$
\text{Only the shaded areas of }II\text{ and }III\text{ are equal.}$
\text{Only the shaded areas of }I\text{ and }III\text{ are equal.}$
\text{The shaded areas of }I, II\text{ and }III\text{ are all different.}

p:
In a sequence of similar geometric diagrams, each larger than the last, the number of shaded smaller units forms a geometric sequence. In the first diagram, there is 1 shaded unit out of 4 total units. In the second diagram, there are 3 shaded units out of 16 total units. If this pattern continues, where the number of shaded units quadruples and the total units quadruple with each new diagram, how many units are shaded in the fourth diagram?
(A) $\frac{1}{5}$
(B) $\frac{1}{4}$
(C) $\frac{3}{5}$
(D) $\frac{1}{3}$

Determine the unique positive integer $m$ such that $$\log_4{(\log_{32}{m})} = \log_8{(\log_8{m})}.$$
$\textbf{(A)}\ 21 \quad \textbf{(B)}\ 22 \quad \textbf{(C)}\ 24 \quad \textbf{(D)}\ 25 \quad \textbf{(E)}\ 26$

Let $r$ be the distance from the origin to a point $P$ with coordinates $(x, y, z)$ in a 3-dimensional space. Define the ratios $\frac{x}{r}$ as $c$, $\frac{y}{r}$ as $s$, and $\frac{z}{r}$ as $z_r$. Compute and analyse the value of $s^2 - c^2 + z_r^2$.
A. 0
B. 1
C. -1
D. between -1 and 1
E. between -1 and 1, including -1 and 1

Find the smallest positive integer greater than 1 that leaves a remainder of 2 when divided by 3, 4, and 5. This number lies between which of the following pairs of numbers?
$\textbf{(A) }2\text{and }29\qquad\textbf{(B) }30\text{and }59\qquad\textbf{(C) }60\text{and }89\qquad\textbf{(D) }90\text{and }119\qquad\textbf{(E) }120\text{and }149$
$\textbf{(A) }2\text{ and }29\qquad\textbf{(B) }30\text{ and }59\qquad\textbf{(C) }60\text{ and }89\qquad\textbf{(D) }90\text{ and }119\qquad\textbf{(E) }120\text{ and }149$

Elmer has recently updated his car. The new car has a $30\%$ improvement in fuel efficiency over his old car but uses diesel fuel, which costs $25\%$ more per liter than the gasoline used by the old car. By what percent does Elmer save or would lose money when using his new car for a long-distance trip?
$\textbf{(A) } 1.23\% \qquad \textbf{(B) } 2.77\% \qquad \textbf{(C) } 3.85\% \qquad \textbf{(D) } 4.56\% \qquad \textbf{(E) } 7.89\%$

Let $ S = \{(x,y) : x \in \{0,1,2,3,4,5\}, y \in \{0,1,2,3\},\text{ and } (x,y)\ne (5,3)\} $. 
Let $ T $ be the set of all right triangles whose vertices are in $ S $. For every right triangle $ t = \triangle{ABC} $ with vertices $ A $, $ B $, and $ C $ in counter-clockwise order and right angle at $ A $, let $ f(t) = \tan(\angle{CBA}) $. What is $$\prod_{t \in T} f(t)?$$
A. $ 1 $
B. $ \frac{1}{2} $
C. $ 2 $
D. $ 3 $
E. $ \frac{5}{144} $

A driver travels for $3$ hours at $65$ miles per hour, during which her car gets $25$ miles per gallon of gasoline. She is paid $0.60$ per mile, and her only expense is gasoline at $2.50$ per gallon. What is her net rate of pay, in dollars per hour, after this expense?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 30\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 31\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 32\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 32.5\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 33$

Two integers have a sum of 28. When two additional integers are added to the first two integers the sum becomes 46. Finally, when two more integers are added to the sum of the previous four integers the sum reaches 66. What is the minimum number of odd integers among these 6 integers?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 1\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 4$

Bernardo and Silvia play a modified game where an integer between $0$ and $999$ inclusive is selected and given to Bernardo. Bernardo triples any number he receives, while Silvia adds $30$ to the number she gets. The game continues until a number exceeds $999$. The winner is the last person who produces a number less than $1000$. Determine the smallest initial number $N$ that results in a win for Bernardo.
(A) 3  
(B) 4  
(C) 5  
(D) 6  
(E) 7

Convert the number 825 into a factorial base of numeration, that is, $825 = a_1 + a_2 \times 2! + a_3 \times 3! + \dots + a_n \times n!$ where $0 \le a_k \le k$. Find the value of $a_5$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 1\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 2\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 3\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 4$

Calculate the arithmetic mean (average) of the first $n$ odd integers. Choose the correct expression for the average:
$\textbf{(A)}\ n \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ n^2 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ n+1 \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ n-1 \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 2n+1$
A) $n$
B) $n^2$
C) $n+1$
D) $n-1$
E) $2n+1$

Michael rode his motorcycle for 40 minutes at a speed of 20 mph, then jogged for 60 minutes at a speed of 5 mph. What was his overall average speed in miles per hour?
A) 8 mph
B) 10 mph
C) 11 mph
D) 14 mph

Triangle $ABC$ and point $P$ in the same plane are given. $\angle APB$ is a right angle, and $\angle APB$ is three times $\angle ACB$. The line segment $\overline{AC}$ intersects $\overline{BP}$ at point $D$. It is known that $PB = 5$ and $PD = 4$. Calculate the product $AD\cdot DC$.
A) 4
B) 7
C) 8
D) 9
E) 10

A quadrilateral has vertices $P(a,b)$, $Q(2b,a)$, $R(-a, -b)$, and $S(-2b, -a)$, where $a$ and $b$ are integers and $a>b>0$. Find the area of quadrilateral $PQRS$.
**A)** $16$ square units 
**B)** $20$ square units 
**C)** $24$ square units 
**D)** $28$ square units 
**E)** $32$ square units

Let $ABCD$ be a rectangle and let $\overline{DM}$ be a segment perpendicular to the plane of $ABCD$. Suppose $\overline{DM}$ has integer length, and the lengths of $\overline{MA},\overline{MB},$ and $\overline{MC}$ are consecutive even positive integers. What is the volume of the pyramid $MABCD$?
A) $8\sqrt{3}$
B) $12\sqrt{3}$
C) $16\sqrt{3}$
D) $24\sqrt{3}$

Let $ g(x) = 5x - x^2 $. Given $ x_0 $, consider the sequence defined by $ x_n = g(x_{n-1}) $ for all $ n \geq 1 $.
Determine how many real numbers $ x_0 $ result in the sequence $ x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots $ taking only a finite number of distinct values.
**A** $0$  
**B** $1$  
**C** $2$  
**D** more than $2$ but finitely many  
**E** infinite

How many unordered pairs of edges in a regular octahedron determine a plane?
$\textbf{(A) } 12 \qquad \textbf{(B) } 36 \qquad \textbf{(C) } 48\qquad \textbf{(D) } 84 \qquad \textbf{(E) } 96$

Find the value of $x + x(x^{x^2})$ when $x = 3$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 59052 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 59053 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 59055 \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 59060 \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 19686$

A half-sector of a circle of radius 6 inches together with its interior is rolled up to form the lateral surface area of a right circular cone by taping together along the two radii shown. What is the volume of the cone in cubic inches?
A) $3\pi\sqrt{5}$
B) $9\pi\sqrt{3}$
C) $6\pi\sqrt{3}$
D) $12\pi\sqrt{3}$
E) $6\pi\sqrt{7}$

Sandwiches at Joe's Fast Food now cost $4 each, and sodas cost $1.50 each. Additionally, there is a $5 discount if you buy at least 10 sandwiches. How many dollars will it cost to purchase 10 sandwiches and 6 sodas?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 43\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 44\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 45\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 46\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 47$

If all dragons are magical creatures and no spooky spirits are dragons, which statement(s) must be true?
$$\textrm{I. All dragons are spooky spirits.}$$
$$\textrm{II. No magical creatures are spooky spirits.}$$
$$\textrm{III. Some magical creatures are not spooky spirits.}$$
$\mathrm{(A)}\ \text{I only} \qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ \text{II only} \qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ \text{III only} \qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ \text{II and III only} \qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ \text{None must be true}$
$\mathrm{(A)}\ \text{I only} \qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ \text{II only} \qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ \text{III only} \qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ \text{II and III only} \qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ \text{None must be true}$
ch:
\text{None must be true}$
$\mathrm{(A)}\ \text{I only} \qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ \text{II only} \qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ \text{III only} \qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ \text{II and III only} \qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ \text{None must be true}

p:
Let $g(x) = \sqrt{3\sin^2{x} + 9 \cos^4{x}} - \sqrt{3\cos^2{x} + 9 \sin^4{x}}.$ Determine the expression equivalent to $g(x)$.
(A) $0$
(B) $\sin x$
(C) $\cos x$
(D) $\sin^2 x$

A cube with a volume of 1 cubic foot is divided into three slabs by making two cuts parallel to the top face of the cube. The first cut is made $\frac{1}{4}$ foot from the top, and the second cut is $\frac{1}{6}$ foot below the first cut. The slabs are labeled consecutively from top to bottom as X, Y, and Z. After cutting, the pieces are reassembled such that they stand end to end in a straight line. Determine the total surface area of the resulting solid assembly.
A. 9 square feet
B. 10 square feet
C. 11 square feet
D. 12 square feet
E. 13 square feet

A school club consists of boys and girls. Initially, $25$ girls leave the club, and then there are three boys for each girl remaining. Following this, $60$ boys leave, after which there are six girls for each boy. Finally, before any of these changes, there was one boy for every girl. Calculate the original number of girls.
A. 55
B. 60
C. 65
D. 67
E. None of these

Let $T$ be the set of ordered triples $(x,y,z)$ of real numbers for which
$$\log_{10}(x+y) = z+1 \text{ and } \log_{10}(x^{2}+y^{2}) = z+2.$$
There are real numbers $c$ and $d$ such that for all ordered triples $(x,y,z)$ in $T$ we have $x^{3}+y^{3}=c \cdot 10^{4z} + d \cdot 10^{3z}.$ What is the value of $c+d?$
A. $\frac{15}{4}$
B. $\frac{28}{2}$
C. $\frac{29}{2}$
D. $\frac{30}{2}$
E. $\frac{31}{2}$

Let $l = 15^2 + 2^{15}$. What is the units digit of $l^2 + 2^l$?
- $\mathrm{(A)}\ 0$
- $\mathrm{(B)}\ 2$
- $\mathrm{(C)}\ 4$
- $\mathrm{(D)}\ 6$
- $\mathrm{(E)}\ 7$

A rug is designed with three differently colored regions, each forming an expanded rectangle, where the areas form an arithmetic progression. The innermost rectangle is 2 feet wide. Each of the two shaded outer regions is now 2 feet wide on all sides (instead of 1 foot). Determine the length of the inner rectangle in feet.
$\textbf{(A) } 2 \qquad \textbf{(B) } 3 \qquad \textbf{(C) } 4 \qquad \textbf{(D) } 5 \qquad \textbf{(E) } 6$

Jeremy rolls three standard six-sided dice, and the product of the numbers on the upper faces is $72$. All dice show different numbers, and at least one die shows a number greater than 4. Which of the following could not be the sum of the upper three faces?
$\mathrm{(A) \ }12 \qquad \mathrm{(B) \ }14 \qquad \mathrm{(C) \ }15 \qquad \mathrm{(D) \ }13 \qquad \mathrm{(E) \ }11$
$\mathrm{(A) \ }12 \qquad \mathrm{(B) \ }14 \qquad \mathrm{(C) \ }15 \qquad \mathrm{(D) \ }13 \qquad \mathrm{(E) \ }11$

An $n$-digit positive integer is adorable if its $n$ digits are an arrangement of the set $\{2, 4, 6, ..., 2n\}$ and its first $k$ digits form an integer that is divisible by $k$, for $k  = 1,2,...,n$. How many adorable $7$-digit integers are there?
$\text{(A)} \ 12684210 \quad \text{(B) } 4261210810 \quad \text{(C) } 4261210814 \quad \text{(D) } 1268421014 \quad \text{(E)} \ 10864212$

A laptop was originally priced at $400.00. It is now offered with successive discounts of 25% followed by an additional 10%. What is the sale price of the laptop as a percentage of its original price?
**A)** 67.5% of 400.00  
**B)** 75% of 400.00   
**C)** 82.5% of 400.00  
**D)** 60% of 400.00  
**E)** none of these

Calculate the sum of the digits of the greatest prime number that is a divisor of $8{,}191$.
A) 3
B) 7
C) 10
D) 16
E) 22

Bob and Alice were initially the same height. Since then, Bob has grown by 25%, while Alice has grown by one third as many inches as Bob. Bob is now 75 inches tall. How tall, in inches, is Alice now?
$\text{(A)}\ 60 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 63 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 65 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 66 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 68$

Four flower beds overlap as shown. Bed A has 600 plants, Bed B has 500 plants, Bed C has 400 plants, and Bed D has 300 plants. Beds A and B share 100 plants, Beds A and C share 150 plants, and Beds B and C share 75 plants. Beds A and D share 50 plants, but there are no overlaps among all four beds. Determine the total number of plants.
(A) 1300
(B) 1350
(C) 1400
(D) 1425
(E) 1450

In the right triangle $PQR$, $PQ=15$, $QR=8$, and angle $R$ is a right angle. A circle is inscribed in the triangle. What is the radius of the inscribed circle?
$\textbf{(A) }1\qquad\textbf{(B) }\frac{3}{2}\qquad\textbf{(C) }\frac{17}{5}\qquad\textbf{(D) }\frac{10}{3}\qquad\textbf{(E) }\frac{60}{13}$

A rancher has $1500 to buy steers at $30 each and cows at $35 each. If the number of steers $s$ and the number of cows $c$ are both positive integers, then what are the feasible solutions?
A) $(s, c) = (1, 42)$  
B) $(s, c) = (36, 12)$  
C) $(s, c) = (71, -18)$  
D) No solution

Billy's basketball team scored the following points over the course of the first $8$ games of the season. If his team scores $50$ in the $9^{th}$ game, which of the following statistics will show an increase?
$$41, 45, 45, 49, 52, 52, 54, 60$$
$\textbf{(A) } \text{range} \qquad \textbf{(B) } \text{median} \qquad \textbf{(C) } \text{mean} \qquad \textbf{(D) } \text{mode} \qquad \textbf{(E) } \text{mid-range}$
$\textbf{(A)}$ Range
$\textbf{(B)}$ Median
$\textbf{(C)}$ Mean
$\textbf{(D)}$ Mode
$\textbf{(E)}$ Mid-range
ch:
text{mid-range}$
$\textbf{(A)}$ Range
$\textbf{(B)}$ Median
$\textbf{(C)}$ Mean
$\textbf{(D)}$ Mode
$\textbf{(E)}$ Mid-range

p:
Two integers have a sum of 30. When two more integers are added to the first two integers the sum is 47. Finally when two more integers are added to the sum of the previous four integers the sum is 65. What is the minimum number of odd integers among the 6 integers?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 1\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 2\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 3\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 4$

Points $A = (2,8)$, $B = (0,0)$, $C = (5,3)$, and $D = (a,b)$ are vertices of quadrilateral $ABCD$ in the first quadrant. The quadrilateral formed by joining the midpoints of $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{BC}$, $\overline{CD}$, and $\overline{DA}$ is a square. Determine the coordinates of point $D$.
A. $(7,3)$
B. $(5,3)$
C. $(4,1)$
D. $(6,2)$

A cylindrical water tank's gauge indicates that there are $60$ liters of water when the tank is $25\%$ full. Additionally, each $5\%$ of the tank's capacity corresponds to $12$ liters. Determine the tank's full capacity in liters.
A) 120 liters
B) 200 liters
C) 240 liters
D) 300 liters

Given a right-angled triangle $I$ with base $(a+b)$ and height equal to $(a+b)$, find the area of a second triangle $II$ with double the area of triangle $I$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 2(a+b)^2\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ (a+b)^2\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ \frac{1}{2}(a+b)^2 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \sqrt{2}(a+b)^2 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 4(a+b)$

A pyramid has a regular hexagonal base with each side of length $2$ and has lateral faces that are isosceles triangles with a vertex angle of $60^\circ$. A regular tetrahedron is placed within the pyramid such that one vertex is at the center of the hexagonal base and the other three vertices touch the midpoints of three non-adjacent edges of the hexagon. What is the volume of this tetrahedron?
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{1}{2} \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}$

The reciprocal of $\left( \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}\right)$ is
(A) $\frac{20}{9}$
(B) $\frac{9}{20}$
(C) $\frac{4}{9}$
(D) $\frac{5}{9}$
(E) $\frac{1}{9}$

A square grid consists of $121$ points arranged in an $11 \times 11$ grid, including points on the edges. Point $P$ is at the center of this square. If point $Q$ is randomly selected from the other $120$ points, what is the probability that line $PQ$ is a line of symmetry for the square?
A) $\frac{1}{4}$  
B) $\frac{1}{3}$  
C) $\frac{1}{2}$  
D) $\frac{2}{3}$  
E) $\frac{3}{4}$

Determine the maximum value of the function $\frac{(2^{t+1} - 4t)t}{16^t}$ for real values of $t$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1}{18} \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{1}{17} \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{1}{16} \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{1}{15} \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \frac{1}{14}$

Angle $B$ of triangle $ABC$ is quadrisectioned by lines $BD$ and $BE$ meeting $AC$ at points $D$ and $E$ respectively, where $D$ is closer to $A$ than $E$. Then determine the ratio $\frac{AD}{EC}$.
(A) $\frac{AB \cdot BD}{BE \cdot BC}$ 
(B) $\frac{AB \cdot BE}{BD \cdot BC}$
(C) $\frac{BE \cdot BD}{AB \cdot BC}$
(D) $\frac{AB}{BE}$ 
(E) $\frac{BD}{BE}$

In our number system the base is ten. If the base were changed to seven, you would count as follows:
$1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 20,\ldots$ The twenty-fifth number would be:
$\textbf{(A)}\ 25_7 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 34_7 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 36_7 \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 104_7 \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 45_7$

Call a fraction $\frac{a}{b}$, not necessarily in the simplest form, special if $a$ and $b$ are positive integers whose sum is $18$. How many distinct integers can be written as the sum of two, not necessarily different, special fractions?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 10 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\  11 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\  12 \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 13 \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 14$

A rectangle $EFGH$ has width $w$ and height $h$. A circle with radius $r$ is tangent to sides $EF$ and $EH$ at their midpoints and also passes through the midpoint of side $FG$. Determine the area of the rectangle in terms of $r$.
A) $2r^2$
B) $4r^2$
C) $8r^2$
D) $16r^2$

The graph of the function $g(x) = x^2 - 2x - 8$ goes:
A) down to the right and up to the left
B) up to the right and down to the left
C) up to the right and up to the left
D) down to the right and down to the left
E) none of these ways.
ch: down to the right and up to the left
up to the right and down to the left
up to the right and up to the left
down to the right and down to the left

p:
Consider the number $450$. Find the two smallest positive integers $x$ and $y$ such that the product of $450$ and $x$ results in a perfect square and the product of $450$ and $y$ results in a perfect cube. What is the sum of $x$ and $y$?
A) 50
B) 62
C) 75
D) 85
E) 100

$\triangle XYZ$ has a right angle at $Z$ and $\angle X = 30^\circ$. If $CZ$ ($C$ on $\overline{XY}$) is the bisector of $\angle XZY$, find $\angle YCZ$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 30^\circ \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 35^\circ \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 40^\circ \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 45^\circ\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 50^\circ$

A circle has a circumference of $18\pi$. Determine the area of this circle in the form of $k\pi$. What is the value of $k$?
$\mathrm{(A)}\ 9 \qquad \mathrm{(B)}\ 36 \qquad \mathrm{(C)}\ 72 \qquad \mathrm{(D)}\ 81 \qquad \mathrm{(E)}\ 144$

A circle is inscribed in a triangle with side lengths $9, 12$, and $15$. Let the segments of the side of length $9$, made by a point of tangency, be $r$ and $s$, with $r<s$. What is the ratio $r:s$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 1:3 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 2:5 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 1:2 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 2:3 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 3:4$

The closed curve in the figure is made up of 12 congruent circular arcs each of length $\frac{\pi}{2}$, where each of the centers of the corresponding circles is among the vertices of a regular hexagon of side 3. What is the area enclosed by the curve?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 12\pi + 13.5\sqrt{3}\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 13.5\sqrt{3} + 3\pi\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 15\sqrt{3} + 2\pi\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 10\sqrt{3} + 4\pi$

Define $x \heartsuit y$ to be $2|x-y|$ for all real numbers $x$ and $y$. Which of the following statements is not true?
- $\mathrm{(A) \ } x \heartsuit y = y \heartsuit x$ for all $x$ and $y$
- $\mathrm{(B) \ } 3(x \heartsuit y) = (3x) \heartsuit (3y)$ for all $x$ and $y$
- $\mathrm{(C) \ } x \heartsuit 0 = 2x$ for all $x \geq 0$
- $\mathrm{(D) \ } x \heartsuit x = 0$ for all $x$
- $\mathrm{(E) \ } x \heartsuit y > 0$ if $x \neq y$
- A) $x \heartsuit y = y \heartsuit x$ for all $x$ and $y$
- B) $3(x \heartsuit y) = (3x) \heartsuit (3y)$ for all $x$ and $y$
- C) $x \heartsuit 0 = 2x$ for all $x \geq 0$
- D) $x \heartsuit x = 0$ for all $x$
- E) $x \heartsuit y > 0$ if $x \neq y$

Jasmine decides to go on a mountain hike. The map shows the total trails she followed during her journey. Calculate Jasmine's average pace in miles per hour for the entire trek.
$\textbf{(A)}4\qquad\textbf{(B)}4.5\qquad\textbf{(C)}5\qquad\textbf{(D)}5.5\qquad\textbf{(E)}6$

How many subsets containing three different numbers can be selected from the set 
$$\{31, 47, 58, 62, 89, 132, 164\}$$
so that the sum of the three numbers is even?
$\text{(A)}\ 4 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 8 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 12 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 16 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 24$

The mean age of Bella's $5$ cousins is $10$, and their median age is $7$. What is the sum of the ages of Bella's youngest and oldest cousins?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 25\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 27\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 29\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 31\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 33$

How many different four-digit numbers can be formed by rearranging the digits in $3622$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 6\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 12\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 24\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 10\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 18$

Circle $A$ passes through the center of, and is tangent to, circle $B$. The area of circle $A$ is $9\pi$ square inches. Determine the area of circle $B$.
A) $18\pi$
B) $36$
C) $36\pi$
D) $72\pi$
E) $72$

A woman has a total of $6000 invested partly at 3.5% and partly at 5.5%. If her annual interest earnings from each portion are equal, calculate the average rate of interest she realizes on her $6000 investment.
**A)** 4.3%  
**B)** 4.5%  
**C)** 4.2%  
**D)** 4.6%  
**E)** None of these

What is the maximum number of cylindrical pieces of clay of radius $2$ and height $5$ that can completely fit inside a cube of side length $9$ assuming the cylinders can be reshaped but not compressed before they are packed in the cube?
$\textbf{(A) }9\qquad\textbf{(B) }10\qquad\textbf{(C) }11\qquad\textbf{(D) }12\qquad\textbf{(E) }13$

There are $6$ coins placed flat on a table. An observer notes the following overlaps: Coin F is on top of all other coins; Coin A is seen covering Coin B and Coin C, but not Coin D or Coin E; Coin D is covering Coin E, which in turn covers Coin B; Coin C is only covered by Coin F and Coin A. Determine the order of the coins from top to bottom.
**$\textbf{(A)}\ (F, A, D, C, E, B)$
$\textbf{(B)}\ (F, A, E, D, B, C)$
$\textbf{(C)}\ (F, A, D, E, C, B)$
$\textbf{(D)}\ (F, A, C, D, E, B)$
$\textbf{(E)}\ (F, A, C, E, D, B)$**

Real numbers $x$, $y$, and $z$ are chosen independently and at random from the interval $[0, m]$ for some positive integer $m$. The probability that no two of $x$, $y$, and $z$ are within 2 units of each other is greater than $\frac{2}{3}$. What is the smallest possible value of $m$?
A) 12
B) 13
C) 14
D) 15
E) 16

Calculate the number of sets of two or more consecutive positive integers whose sum is 150.
- $\textbf{(A)}\ 2$
- $\textbf{(B)}\ 3$
- $\textbf{(C)}\ 4$
- $\textbf{(D)}\ 5$
- $\textbf{(E)}\ 6$

Compare the three fractions $\frac{23}{18}, \frac{21}{16},$ and $\frac{25}{19}$ in order from least to greatest.
A) $\frac{25}{19} < \frac{21}{16} < \frac{23}{18}$
B) $\frac{23}{18} < \frac{25}{19} < \frac{21}{16}$
C) $\frac{21}{16} < \frac{23}{18} < \frac{25}{19}$
D) $\frac{23}{18} < \frac{21}{16} < \frac{25}{19}$
E) $\frac{21}{16} < \frac{25}{19} < \frac{23}{18}$

In a basket of white, orange, and purple marbles, all but 10 are white, all but 12 are orange, and all but 5 are purple. How many marbles are in the basket?
$\textbf{(A)}\hspace{.05in}12\qquad\textbf{(B)}\hspace{.05in}13.5\qquad\textbf{(C)}\hspace{.05in}14\qquad\textbf{(D)}\hspace{.05in}15\qquad\textbf{(E)}\hspace{.05in}15.5$

Mrs. Claudia teaches math to $20$ students. She found that when grading everyone’s test except Barbara’s, the average grade was $78$. After grading Barbara's test, the average grade from all 20 tests increased to $79$. What was Barbara’s score on the test?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 95\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 96\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 98\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 99\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 101$

In a regular hexagon $ABCDEF$, points $G$, $H$, $I$, and $J$ are placed on sides $\overline{BC}$, $\overline{CD}$, $\overline{EF}$, and $\overline{FA}$ respectively such that lines $AB$, $GJ$, $IH$, and $ED$ are parallel and spacings between them are in the ratio 1:2:1 respectively. If the side length of $ABCDEF$ is $a$, calculate the ratio of the area of hexagon $AGIHJF$ to the area of hexagon $ABCDEF$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1}{2}\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{2}{3}\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{3}{4}\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{4}{5}\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ \frac{5}{6}$

Shea and Mika were once the same height. Since then, Shea has grown 25%, while Mika has grown by 10% of her original height. Shea is now 75 inches tall. How tall, in inches, is Mika now?
$\text{(A)}\ 64 \text{ inches} \qquad \text{(B)}\ 66 \text{ inches} \qquad \text{(C)}\ 68 \text{ inches} \qquad \text{(D)}\ 70 \text{ inches} \qquad \text{(E)}\ 72 \text{ inches}$

In quadrilateral $ABCD$, $AB = 7$, $BC = 15$, $CD = 7$, $DA = 11$, and the angle $\angle BDA$ is $90^\circ$. What is the smallest possible integer value of $BD$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 5 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 6 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 7 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 8 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 9$

A bag contains four pieces of paper, each labeled with one of the digits $1$, $2$, $3$, or $5$, with no repeats. Three of these pieces are drawn, one at a time without replacement, to construct a three-digit number. What is the probability that the three-digit number is a multiple of $3$ and also an odd number?
$\textbf{(A)}\ \frac{1}{12}$  
$\textbf{(B)}\ \frac{1}{8}$  
$\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{1}{24}$  
$\textbf{(D)}\ \frac{1}{4}$

A rectangle has an area $A$ and a diagonal $d$. Find the difference between the length and width of the rectangle.
A) $\sqrt{d^2 + 4A}$
B) $\sqrt{d^2 - 2A}$
C) $\sqrt{d^2 - 4A}$
D) $\sqrt{d^2 + 2A}$

An American traveling in Canada wishes to exchange American money (dollars) for Canadian money (dollars). If 120 Canadian dollars are equivalent to 80 American dollars, how much Canadian dollars will the traveler receive in exchange for 50 American dollars?
(A) 60 Canadian dollars
(B) 70 Canadian dollars
(C) 75 Canadian dollars
(D) 85 Canadian dollars
a: The final answer is (C) 75 Canadian dollars.

p:
A warehouse stocks $150$ crates of oranges. Each crate contains at least $130$ oranges and at most $150$ oranges.
What is the largest integer $n$ such that there must be at least $n$ crates containing the same number of oranges?
$\textbf{(A) }6\qquad \textbf{(B) }7\qquad \textbf{(C) }8\qquad \textbf{(D) }9\qquad  \textbf{(E) }10$

A rectangular garden needs to be completely enclosed by a fence. Fence posts are to be placed every 8 meters along the fence, with a post at each corner of the garden. If the dimensions of the garden are 32 meters by 72 meters, what is the fewest number of posts required?
(A) 24 
(B) 25 
(C) 26 
(D) 27 
(E) 28

Let $S_n = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 + \cdots + (-1)^{n-1}n$, where $n$ is a positive integer. Calculate $S_{21} + S_{34} + S_{45}$.
- (A) 15
- (B) 16
- (C) 17
- (D) 18

The number of teeth in four meshed gears $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$ are $w$, $x$, $y$, and $z$, respectively. (The teeth on all gears are the same size and regularly spaced.) If the linear velocity at the point where the gears mesh is constant, find the proportion of their angular speeds in revolutions per minute.
A) $wxyz : wy : wz : wx$
B) $xyz : wz : wyx : wxyz$
C) $xyz : wyz : wxz : wxy$
D) $wx : wy : wz : wxyz$
E) $wxy : wxz : wxyz : wyz$

Eva and her four sisters have ages 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10. One afternoon, two of her sisters, whose ages sum to 12, went to the park. The oldest sister and the youngest sister went to a concert, and Eva, along with a 4-year-old sister, stayed home. How old is Eva?
**A.** 2  
**B.** 4  
**C.** 6  
**D.** 8  
**E.** 10

A palindrome between $1000$ and $10,000$ is chosen at random. What is the probability that it is divisible by $11$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ \dfrac{1}{10} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ \dfrac{1}{9} \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ \dfrac{1}{7} \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \dfrac{1}{6} \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \dfrac{1}{5}$

Let $ x = \frac{-1 + i\sqrt{3}}{2} $ and $ y = \frac{-1 - i\sqrt{3}}{2} $, where $ i^2 = -1 $. Determine the smallest positive integer $ n $ for which $ x^n + y^n = 2 $.
- A) 3
- B) 6
- C) 9
- D) 12
- E) 15

Suppose $m$ is the product of four consecutive integers and that $m$ is divisible by $11$. Which of the following is not necessarily a divisor of $m$?
- $8$
- $22$
- $33$
- $44$
- $66$
- A: 8
- B: 22
- C: 33
- D: 44
- E: 66

A box contains $4$ pennies, $5$ nickels, and $7$ dimes. Eight coins are drawn without replacement, with each coin having an equal probability of being chosen. What is the probability that the value of the coins drawn is at least $60$ cents?
- $\text{(A)} \ \frac{631}{12870}$
- $\text{(B)} \ \frac{600}{12870}$
- $\text{(C)} \ \frac{750}{12870}$
- $\text{(D)}\ \frac{500}{12870}$

How many ordered pairs $(m,n)$ of positive integers, with $m > n$, have the property that their squares differ by $120$?
A) 3
B) 4
C) 5
D) 6
E) 8

A majority of the $40$ students in Mr. Figures' class bought pens at the school store. Each of these students bought the same number of pens, and this number was an odd number greater than $1$. The cost of a pen in cents was a prime number, and the total cost of all the pens was $$20.91$. What was the cost of a pen in cents?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 3 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 7 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 47 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 2091 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 23$

Which of the following sets includes all values of $x$ that satisfy the inequality $3x - 5 \geq 9 - 2x$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ x > \frac{14}{5} \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ x < \frac{14}{5} \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ x \geq \frac{14}{5} \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ x \leq \frac{14}{5}\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ x = \frac{14}{5}$

In an arithmetic sequence, the first term is $5$, the last term is $50$, and the sum of all the terms is $275$. Determine the common difference.
$\text{(A) } 5 \qquad \text{(B) } 10 \qquad \text{(C) } 4.5 \qquad \text{(D) } 9$

Letters $A, B, C,$ and $D$ represent four different non-zero digits selected from $1, 2, \ldots, 9$. What is the maximum value of the expression $(A+B) + (C+D)$?
A) 28
B) 29
C) 30
D) 31
E) 32

John counted the number of edges of a tetrahedron, Jane counted the numbers of corners, and Jill counted the number of faces. They then added the three numbers. What was the resulting sum?
$\mathrm{(A)}\ 10 \qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ 12 \qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ 14 \qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ 16 \qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ 18$

There is a list of seven numbers. The average of the first four numbers is $7$, and the average of the last four numbers is $9$. If the average of all seven numbers is $8$, then the number common to both sets of four numbers is
A) $7$
B) $7.5$
C) $8$
D) $8.5$
E) $9$

Each of the $18$ balls is tossed independently and at random into one of the $4$ bins. Let $p$ be the probability that one bin ends up with $6$ balls, another with $2$ balls, and the other two with $5$ balls each. Let $q$ be the probability that two bins end up with $5$ balls each, and the other two with $4$ balls each. What is $\frac{p}{q}$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 3 \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 4 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ \frac{10}{3} \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 6 \qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 8$

Ms. Henderson states that to secure an A in her course, a student must score at least 90% on the multiple-choice section of her exam. Which of the following statements necessarily follows logically from this information?
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{If Robert did not receive an A, then he scored less than 90% on the multiple-choice questions.}\\
\textbf{(B)}\ \text{If Robert did not receive an A, then he scored below 50% on the multiple-choice questions.}\\
\textbf{(C)}\ \text{If Robert scored at least 90% on the multiple-choice questions, then he received an A.}\\
\textbf{(D)}\ \text{If Robert received an A, then he scored at least 95% on the multiple-choice questions.}\\
\textbf{(E)}\ \text{If Robert received an A, then he scored at least 90% on the multiple-choice questions.}$

\textbf{(B)}\ \text{If Robert did not receive an A, then he scored below 50% on the multiple-choice questions.}\\
\textbf{(C)}\ \text{If Robert scored at least 90% on the multiple-choice questions, then he received an A.}\\

\textbf{(B)}\ \text{If Robert did not receive an A, then he scored below 50% on the multiple-choice questions.}\\
\textbf{(C)}\ \text{If Robert scored at least 90% on the multiple-choice questions, then he received an A.}\\
\textbf{(D)}\ \text{If Robert received an A, then he scored at least 95% on the multiple-choice questions.}\\
\textbf{(E)}\ \text{If Robert received an A, then he scored at least 90% on the multiple-choice questions.}

p:
Rectangle ABCD has dimensions AB=CD=4 and BC=AD=8. The rectangle is rotated 90° clockwise about corner D, then rotated 90° clockwise about the corner C's new position after the first rotation. What is the length of the path traveled by point A?
A) $8\sqrt{5}\pi$  
B) $4\sqrt{5}\pi$  
C) $2\sqrt{2}\pi$  
D) $4\sqrt{2}\pi$

Given a circle with center $O$ and radius $r$, and two parallel tangents which are at a distance $d$ from $O$ where $d > r$, find the number of points equidistant from the circle and both tangents.
A) 0
B) 2
C) 3
D) 4
E) Infinite

A checkerboard of $15$ rows and $19$ columns has a number written in each square, beginning in the upper left corner, so that the first row is numbered $3, 4, \ldots, 21$, the second row $22, 23, \ldots, 40$, and so on down the board. If the board is renumbered so that the left column, top to bottom, is $3, 4, \ldots, 17,$, the second column $18, 19, \ldots, 32$ and so on across the board, determine the sum of the numbers in these squares that have the same numbers in both numbering systems.
A) 387
B) 440
C) 502
D) 555
E) 612

A circular region has a radius of 4 units. Determine the probability that a point randomly chosen within this circle is closer to the center than it is to a point that is exactly 3 units away from the boundary.
(A) $\frac{1}{4}$
(B) $\frac{1}{16}$
(C) $\frac{1}{8}$
(D) $\frac{1}{32}$
(E) $\frac{1}{2}$

The expression $\sqrt{16-t^2}+3$ equals zero for:
$\textbf{(A)}\ \text{no real or imaginary values of }t\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ \text{no real values of }t\text{ only}\\ \textbf{(C)}\ \text{no imaginary values of }t\text{ only}\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ t=0\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ t=\pm \sqrt{7}$

In the Arden Cricket Club, each member purchases gloves and caps. Gloves cost $6 each and caps cost $8 more than a pair of gloves. Each member requires one pair of gloves and one cap for the match session, and another pair of gloves and cap for the practice session. If the total expenditure of the club is $4140, determine how many members are there in the club.
(A) 99
(B) 101
(C) 103
(D) 105
(E) 107
a: Provide the correct answer option based on the choices.

p:
Estimate to determine which of the following numbers is closest to $\frac{805}{0.410}$.
$\text{(A)}\ .4 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 4 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 40 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 400 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 4000$
$\text{(A)}\ .4 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 4 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 40 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 400 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 4000$

An amusement park has a collection of scale models, with a ratio of $1: 30$, of buildings and other sights from around the country. The height of the Statue of Liberty, excluding its pedestal, is $305$ feet. What is the height in feet of its duplicate to the nearest whole number?
$\textbf{(A) }9\qquad\textbf{(B) }10\qquad\textbf{(C) }11\qquad\textbf{(D) }12\qquad\textbf{(E) }13$

Consider several sets of prime numbers such as $\{1063, 2789, 41, 527\}$ which use each of the ten digits exactly once. What is the smallest possible sum such a set could have?
$\text{(A) }4420 \qquad \text{(B) }4500 \qquad \text{(C) }4650 \qquad \text{(D) }4700 \qquad \text{(E) }4800$

In a particular card game variant, a player is dealt a hand of $12$ cards from a deck consisting of $60$ unique cards. The number of distinct (unordered) ways this hand can be dealt needs to be divisible by $6$. The number, when this condition is satisfied, is expressed as $1BCB529080B$. What is the digit $B$?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 3 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 5 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 7 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 9 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 2$

Using each of the digits 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, twice, create four five-digit numbers. What is the largest possible sum of these four numbers by strategically placing the digits?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 150000 \quad \textbf{(B)}\ 150628 \quad \textbf{(C)}\ 150500 \quad \textbf{(D)}\ 151000 \quad \textbf{(E)}\ 152000$

What is the tens digit of $1047^{1024}-1049?$
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 1 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 3 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 5 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 8$
$\textbf{(A)}\ 0 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 1 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 3 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 5 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 8$

Let $A$, $B$ and $C$ be three distinct points on the graph of $y=2x^2$ such that line $AB$ is parallel to the $x$-axis and $\triangle ABC$ is an isosceles right triangle with area $1600$. Find the sum of the digits of the $y$-coordinate of $C$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 5\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 7\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 9\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 11\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 13$

Given four positive integers, for each integer, sum the other three integers, divide by three, and add 10 to the result. This process results in the numbers $37$, $31$, $25$, and $19$. Find one of the original integers.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 27 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 45 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 72 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 37 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 10$

The number of terms in an arithmetic progression (A.P.) is even. The sum of the odd-numbered terms is 36, and the sum of the even-numbered terms is 45. If the last term exceeds the first term by 15. If the common difference is a whole number, find the number of terms in the A.P.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 6 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 12 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 8 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 10 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 14$

Two circles have centers at points $A$ and $B$ respectively, with radii in the ratio 1:2. Each circle passes through the other's center. The line containing both $A$ and $B$ is extended to intersect the circles at points $C$ and $D$, respectively. The circles intersect at two points, one of which is $E$. Determine the degree measure of $\angle CED$.
A) 90°
B) 105°
C) 120°
D) 135°
E) 150°

The marked price of a book was 40% less than the suggested retail price. Alice purchased the book for 60% of the marked price during a special sale. What percent of the suggested retail price did Alice pay?
$\mathrm{(A)}\ 24\%\qquad\mathrm{(B)}\ 30\%\qquad\mathrm{(C)}\ 36\%\qquad\mathrm{(D)}\ 40\%\qquad\mathrm{(E)}\ 50\%$

Bridget bought a bag of apples. She first gave 4 apples to her brother Tom. Then, she split the remaining apples equally between her friends Ann and Cassie. After distributing the apples, she was left with 5 apples. How many apples did Bridget buy originally?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 9\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 11\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 13\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 15\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 17$

A project requires 10 workers to complete it in 15 days. If 5 additional workers join after the first 5 days, how long in total will it take to complete the project?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 6 \text{ days}\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 7 \text{ days}\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 10 \text{ days}\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 20 \text{ days}$

Five years ago, John was twice as old as his sister Mary. Seven years before that, John was three times as old as Mary. In how many years will the ratio of their ages be $3$ : $2$?
A) 6
B) 8
C) 9
D) 10
E) 12

Given constants $a > 0$ and $d$, for each real number $t$, let $(x_t, y_t)$ be the vertex of the parabola $y = ax^2 + tx + d$. Determine the type of graph formed by the set of vertices $(x_t, y_t)$ as $t$ varies over all real numbers.
A) a straight line
B) a parabola
C) part, but not all, of a parabola
D) one branch of a hyperbola
E) None of these
ch: part, but not all, of a parabola

p:
For each positive integer $ n $, let $ T(n) $ be the number of sequences of length $ n $ consisting solely of the letters $ C $ and $ D $, with no more than two $ C $s in a row and no more than two $ D $s in a row. What is the remainder when $ T(10) $ is divided by $ 5 $?
A) 0
B) 1
C) 2
D) 3
E) 4

Real numbers are chosen between 0 and 1, inclusive, in the following manner. A biased coin with a probability $p$ of landing heads is flipped. If it lands heads, then it is flipped again: the chosen number is 0 if the second flip is heads, and 1 if the second flip is tails. If the first coin flip is tails, then the number is chosen by flipping the coin again: if heads, the number is $\frac{1}{4}$; if tails, the number is $\frac{3}{4}$. Two random numbers $x$ and $y$ are chosen independently in this manner. What is the probability that $|x-y| > \frac{1}{2}$?
$\textbf{(A)} \frac{1}{4} \quad \textbf{(B)} \frac{3}{16} \quad \textbf{(C)} \frac{1}{8} \quad \textbf{(D)} \frac{1}{2} \quad \textbf{(E)} \frac{1}{3}$

Make a lattice using toothpicks that forms a rectangle 30 toothpicks long and 40 toothpicks wide. Additionally, one diagonal is laid across the rectangle. How many toothpicks are used in total?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 2510 \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 2520 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 2530 \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 2540 \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 2550$

The $7$-digit numbers $\underline{8} \underline{5} \underline{A} \underline{3} \underline{6} \underline{B} \underline{4}$ and $\underline{9} \underline{1} \underline{7} \underline{B} \underline{A} \underline{5} \underline{C}$ are each multiples of $11$. Which of the following could be the value of $C$?
$\textbf{(A) }0\qquad\textbf{(B) }2\qquad\textbf{(C) }5\qquad\textbf{(D) }7\qquad \textbf{(E) }9$
$\textbf{(A) }0\qquad\textbf{(B) }2\qquad\textbf{(C) }5\qquad\textbf{(D) }7\qquad \textbf{(E) }9$

Homer started peeling a pile of 50 potatoes at the rate of 4 potatoes per minute. Five minutes later, Christen joined him and peeled at the rate of 6 potatoes per minute. When they finished, how many potatoes had Christen peeled?
$\text{(A)}\ 15 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 18 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 21 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 24 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 27$

Using the same rules and a different grid arrangement of identical characters BMC1, calculate how many different paths spell BMC1 starting from the central 'B'. Move only to adjacent letters (up, down, left, right, excluding diagonal).
(A) 8
(B) 12
(C) 16
(D) 24
(E) 32

Main Street runs east-west, and a railroad track runs north-south, dividing a larger triangular piece of land. The triangular plot RST is situated between Raven Road, Swan Road, and the railroad. The distance along Main Street from R to T (base of triangle RST) is 5 miles. The height from point S to line RT (along Swan Road) is 4 miles. Within this triangle, there is a smaller triangle RSC, with RC along Raven Road measuring 1 mile, and the height from point S to line RC along Swan Road is 4 miles. Find the area of the plot of land RST minus the area of triangle RSC.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 6\qquad \textbf{(B)}\ 7\qquad \textbf{(C)}\ 8\qquad \textbf{(D)}\ 10\qquad \textbf{(E)}\ 12$

In rectangle $ABCD$ with length 2 and height 1, an inscribed circle $\omega$ intersects $\overline{AB}$ at $Q$ (with $A$ at the top left corner and $B$ at the top right corner of the rectangle). Point $D$ is at the bottom left corner. Line segment $\overline{DQ}$ intersects circle $\omega$ again at a point $R$ different from $Q$. Find the length of $DR$.
$\textbf{(A) } 0 \qquad \textbf{(B) } 0.5 \qquad \textbf{(C) } 1.0 \qquad \textbf{(D) } 1.5 \qquad \textbf{(E) } 2.0$

A school club buys 800 candy bars at a price of six for $3 dollars. They sell all the candy bars at the price of three for $2 dollars. In addition, there is a tax of 10% on the total sales revenue. What was their profit, after tax, in dollars?
$\textbf{(A) }\ 70.02      \qquad \textbf{(B) }\ 80.02      \qquad \textbf{(C) }\ 90.02      \qquad \textbf{(D) }\ 100.02      \qquad \textbf{(E) }\ 110.02$

Pegs are placed in a board $1$ unit apart both horizontally and vertically. A rubber band is stretched over $4$ pegs, forming a new quadrilateral, with vertices at points (1, 1), (2, 3), (4, 2), and (3, 0). Calculate its area in square units.
A) $4.5$
B) $5$
C) $5.5$
D) $6$
E) $6.5$

An item's price is increased by 50%, and the seller wants to ensure that the overall profit increases by 20% despite a possible decrease in demand. What percentage decrease in demand can occur while still achieving this profit increase?
A. 10%
B. 15%
C. 20%
D. 25%

For which non-zero real numbers $x$ is $\frac{|x-3|x||}{x}$ a positive integer?
A) 1
B) 2
C) 3
D) 4

In triangle $ABC$, $BD$ bisects $\angle ABC$ and $BE$ (where $E$ is on line segment $AC$) bisects $\angle DBA$. Then, determine the correct relationship between the segments formed on $AC$.
A) $\frac{AD}{DC} = \frac{AB}{BC}$
B) $\frac{AD}{DC} = \frac{BD}{BE}$
C) $\frac{AD}{DC} = \frac{AE}{AB}$
D) $\frac{AD}{DC} = \frac{BC}{AB}$

Given a number line where markings are equally spaced, calculate the number $y$. If $y$ is reached after taking four steps from $0$, and the endpoint at six steps is $24$, what is the number $y$?
- $\text{(A)}\ 8$
- $\text{(B)}\ 12$
- $\text{(C)}\ 16$
- $\text{(D)}\ 20$
- $\text{(E)}\ 24$

A half-sector of a circle with a radius of 6 inches is rolled up to form the lateral surface area of a right circular cone by taping along the two radii. What is the volume of the cone in cubic inches?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 6\pi \sqrt{3} \qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 9\pi \sqrt3 \qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 12\pi \sqrt{3} \qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 15\pi \sqrt3$

How many of the numbers, $1000,1001,\cdots,9999$ have four different digits in increasing order or in decreasing order?
A) 240
B) 252
C) 280
D) 300
E) 336

John is buying six items at a hardware store. The prices of the items are $12.50$, $3.25$, $7.75$, $9.40$, $5.60$, and $2.15$. John pays with a fifty-dollar bill. Which of the following is closest to the percentage of the $50.00$ that he will receive in change?
$\text{(A) } 15\% \qquad \text{(B) } 18\% \qquad \text{(C) } 20\% \qquad \text{(D) } 25\% \qquad \text{(E) } 30\%$

If $f(x) = \frac{x(x-1)}{2}$, then $f(x+3)$ equals:
$\textbf{(A)}\ (x+3)f(x) \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ f(x)+x+3 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ f(x+1)+f(x+2) \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \frac{(x+3)f(x+1)}{x} \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \frac{(x+3)(f(x+1)+f(x+2))}{x}$
$\textbf{(A)}\ (x+3)f(x) \qquad \textbf{(B)}\ f(x)+x+3 \qquad \textbf{(C)}\ f(x+1)+f(x+2) \qquad \textbf{(D)}\ \frac{(x+3)f(x+1)}{x} \qquad \textbf{(E)}\ \frac{(x+3)(f(x+1)+f(x+2))}{x}$

How many whole numbers from 1 through 51 are divisible by either 4 or 6 or both?
$\text{(A)}\ 14 \qquad \text{(B)}\ 15 \qquad \text{(C)}\ 16 \qquad \text{(D)}\ 17 \qquad \text{(E)}\ 18$

If $m>0$ and the points $(m,4)$ and $(2,m)$ lie on a line with slope $2m$, then find the value of $m=$
A) $\frac{1 + \sqrt{17}}{2}$
B) $\frac{3 + \sqrt{41}}{4}$
C) $\sqrt{5}$
D) $\frac{5 + \sqrt{65}}{7}$

The sum of eight integers is 0. What is the maximum number of these eight integers that can be larger than 15?
$\textbf{(A)}\ 5\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 6\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 7\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 8\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 9$

Evaluate the expression $\frac{3x^2+x}{(x-1)(x+3)} + \frac{5-x}{(x-1)(x+3)}$ for values of $ x $ where it is defined.
A) $\frac{3x^2+4}{(x+1)(x-3)}$
B) $\frac{3x^2+5}{(x-1)(x+3)}$
C) $\frac{3x^2+4}{(x-1)(x+3)}$
D) $\frac{x^2+5}{(x-1)(x+3)}$

Consider a dartboard painted over a rectangular clock face with the dimensions ratio of length to width being 2:1, using only the 3, 6, 9, and 12 "hour positions" as boundaries. Determine the ratio of the area of one of the rectangle cut by the 3 and 9 o'clock marks from the center (let's denote this as $p$), and the area of one of the triangles formed by the intersections at 12 and 6 o'clock with one of the corners (let's denote this as $r$). Calculate $\frac{p}{r}$.
A) $\frac{1}{2}$
B) $2$
C) $4$
D) $8$

Let $n$ be a 5-digit number, and let $q$ and $r$ be the quotient and the remainder, respectively, when $n$ is divided by $50$. For how many values of $n$ is $q+r$ divisible by $7$?
$\mathrm{(A) \ } 14000\qquad \mathrm{(B) \ } 14400\qquad \mathrm{(C) \ } 15000\qquad \mathrm{(D) \ } 16000\qquad \mathrm{(E) \ } 17000$

Determine the roots of the equation $3\sqrt{x} + 3x^{-\frac{1}{2}} = 8$.
**A)** $\left(\frac{8 + 2\sqrt{7}}{6}\right)^2, \left(\frac{8 - 2\sqrt{7}}{6}\right)^2$
**B)** $\left(\frac{8 + \sqrt{7}}{6}\right)^2, \left(\frac{8 - \sqrt{7}}{6}\right)^2$
**C)** $\left(\frac{7 + 2\sqrt{8}}{6}\right)^2, \left(\frac{7 - 2\sqrt{8}}{6}\right)^2$
**D)** $\left(\frac{9 + 2\sqrt{6}}{6}\right)^2, \left(\frac{9 - 2\sqrt{6}}{6}\right)^2$

A sequence of numbers is defined recursively by $b_1 = 2$, $b_2 = \frac{5}{11}$, and
$$b_n = \frac{b_{n-2} \cdot b_{n-1}}{3b_{n-2} - 2b_{n-1}}$$ for all $n \geq 3$. Calculate $b_{10}$ and express it as $\frac{p}{q}$, where $p$ and $q$ are relatively prime positive integers. What is $p+q$?
A) 4
B) 5
C) 6
D) 7

Let $ x = -2023 $. Calculate the value of $\left\vert \left\vert \left\vert x \right\vert - x \right\vert + \left\vert x \right\vert \right\vert - x$.
$\textbf{(A)}\ 6069\qquad\textbf{(B)}\ 4046\qquad\textbf{(C)}\ 8092\qquad\textbf{(D)}\ 2023\qquad\textbf{(E)}\ 10092$

In a certain tiling pattern, each row is constructed with alternating blue and green square tiles, beginning and ending with a blue tile. The first row has a single blue tile and each subsequent row has two more tiles than the previous. Determine the number of green tiles in the 50th row.
A) 48
B) 49
C) 50
D) 51
E) 52

Two concentric circles have radii of 15 meters and 30 meters respectively. An aardvark starts at point A on the smaller circle, travels half the circumference of the smaller circle to point B, moves radially out to point C on the larger circle, travels half the circumference of the larger circle to point D, and finally returns to the center (point E). Calculate the total distance the aardvark travels.
**A)** $15\pi + 45\qquad$
**B)** $30\pi + 60\qquad$
**C)** $45\pi + 45\qquad$
**D)** $60\pi + 30\qquad$
**E)** $75\pi + 15$

If $ n $ and $ m $ are integers and $ n^2 + m^2 $ is odd, which of the following must be true?
1. $ n $ and $ m $ are both odd
2. $ n $ and $ m $ are both even
3. $ nm $ is even
4. $ nm $ is odd
A) $ n $ and $ m $ are both odd
B) $ n $ and $ m $ are both even
C) $ nm $ is even
D) $ nm $ is odd
ch: ( n $ and $ m $ are both odd
( n $ and $ m $ are both even

p:
The ratio of the number of games won to the number of games lost by the Riverdale Rangers is $8/5$. If the team played a total of 65 games, what percent of its games did the team lose?
$\text{(A)}\ 35\% \qquad \text{(B)}\ 38\% \qquad \text{(C)}\ 42\% \qquad \text{(D)}\ 45\% \qquad \text{(E)}\ 50\%$

Consider a quadrilateral bounded by the graphs of $(x - ay)^2 = 9a^2$ and $(ax + y)^2 = 4a^2$, where $a > 0$ is a positive real number. Determine the area of this region in terms of $a$.
$\textbf{(A)} ~\frac{12a^2}{1+a^2}\qquad\textbf{(B)} ~\frac{24a^2}{1+a^2}\qquad\textbf{(C)} ~\frac{18a^2}{1+a^2}\qquad\textbf{(D)} ~\frac{24a^2}{a^2+1}\qquad\textbf{(E)} ~\frac{30a^2}{1+a^2}$

Assume $p$ and $q$ are primes and $x^2 - 2px + pq = 0$ has distinct integral roots. Evaluate the following statements:
$I.\ \text{The sum of the roots is even.}$ 
$II.\ \text{Neither root is prime.}$
$III.\ p + q\ \text{is prime.}$
$IV.\ pq - p\ \text{is prime.}$
$\textbf{(A)}\ I\ \text{only}$
$\textbf{(B)}\ II\ \text{only}$
$\textbf{(C)}\ III\ \text{only}$
$\textbf{(D)}\ IV\ \text{only}$
$\textbf{(E)}\ I\ \text{and}\ III\ \text{only}$

A hexagonal dart board is divided into regions as shown. Suppose that a dart thrown at the board is equally likely to land anywhere on the board. What is the probability that the dart lands within the center hexagon?

The dart board consists of a central hexagon and six equilateral triangles, one at each side of the hexagon. All the triangles are congruent, and their sides are equal to the sides of the hexagon.
A) $\frac{1}{4}$  
B) $\frac{1}{3}$  
C) $\frac{1}{2}$  
D) $\frac{2}{3}$  
E) $\frac{3}{4}$

The sum of the digits of a two-digit number is subtracted from the number. The units digit of the result is $4$. How many two-digit numbers have this property?
$\textbf{(A) } 5\qquad \textbf{(B) } 7\qquad \textbf{(C) } 9\qquad \textbf{(D) } 10\qquad \textbf{(E) } 19$

The lines $x=\frac{1}{3}y+a$ and $y=\frac{1}{3}x+b$ intersect at the point $(2,3)$. Determine the value of $a+b$.
A) $\frac{8}{3}$
B) $4$
C) $\frac{9}{3}$
D) $\frac{10}{3}$

Evaluate the expression $2^{-(3k+1)}-3 \cdot 2^{-(3k-1)}+4 \cdot 2^{-3k}$.
$\textbf{(A) } -\frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{-3k} \qquad \textbf{(B) } \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{-3k} \qquad \textbf{(C) } -\frac{5}{2} \cdot 2^{-3k} \qquad \textbf{(D) } \frac{5}{2} \cdot 2^{-3k}$

Determine how many ordered triples $(x, y, z)$ of integers satisfy the system of equations:
